title: Fallback Providers
summary: Three-layer resilience system for Hermes: credential pools (same-provider key rotation), primary model fallback (cross-provider automatic failover on errors), and auxiliary task fallback (independent provider resolution for vision, compression, web extraction, etc.).

config_keys.description: Ordered list of fallback provider+model pairs (preferred key)
Legacy single-fallback key (still honored for back-compat)

code_examples.caption: OpenRouter as fallback for Anthropic
code_examples.code:
model:
  provider: anthropic
  default: claude-sonnet-4-6

fallback_model:
  provider: openrouter
  model: anthropic/claude-sonnet-4

code_examples.caption: Custom endpoint fallback
code_examples.code:
fallback_model:
  provider: custom
  model: my-local-model
  base_url: http://localhost:8000/v1
  key_env: MY_LOCAL_KEY

code_examples.caption: Auxiliary task config
code_examples.code:
auxiliary:
  vision:
    provider: "auto"
    model: ""
  compression:
    provider: "auto"
    model: "google/gemini-3-flash-preview"
  session_search:
    provider: main
    model: glm-4.5-air
    max_concurrency: 2

gotchas: Fallback is per-turn, not per-session — primary model is restored at the start of each new user message
Within a single turn, fallback activates at most once — if fallback also fails, normal error handling takes over
Subagents spawned by delegate_task do NOT inherit the primary fallback model
Cron jobs do not support fallback — use per-job provider overrides
fallback_providers (plural list) takes priority over fallback_model (singular) when both are set
No environment variables for fallback_model — configured exclusively through config.yaml
full_text:
# Fallback Providers

Hermes Agent has three layers of resilience that keep your sessions running when providers hit issues.
